author: Dr. Robert Kim
hpi:
69-year-old male presents for routine urology follow-up to evaluate benign prostatic hyperplasia management. Patient was seen for BPH initially in 2016 and has been maintained on tamsulosin 0.4mg daily since that time. He reports that his lower urinary tract symptoms have been relatively stable over the past few months. He still experiences a weak urinary stream and hesitancy, particularly first thing in the morning, but this has not significantly worsened. Nocturia has been 2 times per night, improved from the 3-4 episodes reported in January after adjusting his evening furosemide timing to 4 PM.

He denies gross hematuria, dysuria, urinary retention requiring catheterization, or urinary incontinence. He had a UTI in the past but not recently. He notes that the furosemide for his heart failure significantly contributes to his urinary frequency during the daytime, voiding approximately every 2-3 hours, but he has adapted to this. AUA symptom score today: 14 (moderate, slightly up from 12 at diagnosis in 2016).

Of note, patient was recently hospitalized in February 2021 for community-acquired pneumonia. He was treated with antibiotics and has since made a full recovery. Follow-up CXR on 3/1 was clear. He has returned to his baseline functional status. Heart failure remains compensated on current GDMT.

reviewOfSystems.Constitutional: No fevers or chills. Energy level has returned to baseline after pneumonia hospitalization. Weight stable at 99.5 kg.
reviewOfSystems.Cardiovascular: Baseline dyspnea on exertion with moderate activity (NYHA II). No chest pain, orthopnea, or PND. No lower extremity swelling worsening.
reviewOfSystems.Respiratory: Cough from pneumonia fully resolved. No hemoptysis or wheezing.
reviewOfSystems.GI: Appetite good, improved after illness. Regular bowel movements. No abdominal pain. Following low-sodium diet.
reviewOfSystems.GU: Weak stream, hesitancy, nocturia x2 as described. No hematuria. No dysuria. No incontinence. No history of urinary retention.
reviewOfSystems.Musculoskeletal: No back pain or pelvic pain.
reviewOfSystems.Neurologic: Bilateral foot numbness from diabetic neuropathy, unchanged. Reports tingling worse at night.
reviewOfSystems.Skin: No genital or perineal rashes or lesions.

physicalExam.General: Alert, oriented, pleasant 69-year-old male in no acute distress. Well-nourished, obese habitus (BMI ~32).
physicalExam.HEENT: Not examined in detail — deferred to PCP.
physicalExam.Cardiovascular: Irregularly irregular rhythm (known A-fib). Rate ~67 bpm. No murmurs appreciated on focused exam. Trace bilateral ankle edema.
physicalExam.Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles. Lungs clear, consistent with resolution of recent pneumonia.
physicalExam.Abdomen: Soft, obese, non-tender. No suprapubic fullness or tenderness. No costovertebral angle tenderness. Bladder not palpable after voiding.
physicalExam.GU/DRE: External genitalia normal male, circumcised, no lesions. Digital rectal exam: prostate moderately enlarged (estimated 45g), smooth, symmetric, no nodules, no tenderness. Median sulcus palpable. Rectal tone normal.
physicalExam.Extremities: 1+ pitting edema bilateral ankles. Pedal pulses 1+ bilaterally. Decreased sensation to monofilament bilateral plantar surfaces (known neuropathy).
physicalExam.Neurologic: Alert and oriented. Decreased sensation bilateral feet as noted.

assessment.notes: AUA symptom score 14 (moderate), slightly increased from 12 at diagnosis in 2016 but not significantly bothersome to patient. DRE shows moderately enlarged prostate without nodules. PSA 2.3 in December 2020 (stable). Currently managed with tamsulosin 0.4mg daily with adequate symptomatic control. Given his multiple medications and comorbidities (HFrEF, CKD), adding a 5-alpha reductase inhibitor is an option if symptoms progress, but not warranted at this time.

assessment.diagnosis: Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction
assessment.notes: NYHA Class II, compensated on GDMT. Recent pneumonia hospitalization in February — recovered fully. Contributing to urinary frequency via diuretic use (furosemide 40mg BID), which complicates assessment of LUTS severity.
A1c 7.6% per recent labs. Neuropathy may contribute to bladder dysfunction over time — will monitor for neurogenic bladder symptoms.

plan.action: Continue tamsulosin 0.4mg daily — adequate symptomatic control at present. AUA score 14 is moderate but patient not requesting additional intervention. Discussed option of adding finasteride if symptoms worsen or prostate enlarges further, but patient prefers to continue current regimen. Reviewed importance of reporting any hematuria, retention, or significant worsening of symptoms.
PSA 2.3 in December — stable and within normal limits. Repeat PSA in 12 months. No indication for prostate biopsy at this time.

Daytime frequency largely driven by furosemide. Timing adjustment of PM dose to 4 PM has helped nocturnal symptoms. No further intervention needed from urology standpoint. Coordinated with PCP and cardiology regarding diuretic management.

No current evidence of neurogenic bladder or overflow incontinence. Post-void residual not measured today as patient voided adequately. Will check PVR at next visit if symptoms change. If retention develops, may need intermittent catheterization training.
Return to urology in 6 months. Repeat PSA at that time. Sooner if hematuria, retention, or significant symptom progression.

attestation:
I personally saw and examined the patient. I agree with the documented findings and plan.

Electronically signed by: Dr. Robert Kim
Date: 4/8/2021
